name: En Shi Yu Lu
terroir: Selenium-rich soil in the Wuling Mountains, ~800-1200m, subtropical monsoon climate with heavy fog
flavor_profile: China's most famous steamed green tea and one of very few surviving examples of the Tang-dynasty steaming method. The steaming preserves more chlorophyll and amino acids than pan-firing, producing a vivid emerald liquor with a mellow, full-bodied umami character. The flavor is smoother and more marine-vegetal than pan-fired greens — closer in spirit to Japanese gyokuro, though with its own distinct sweetness and mineral depth from Enshi's selenium-rich terroir.

brewing.tips: As a steamed green, it's more temperature-sensitive than pan-fired teas. Start at 80°C; going lower (even 70°C) brings out more umami at the cost of body.
